skill.name: gget
skill.description: Use gget for rapid bioinformatics lookups across genomic, protein, expression, enrichment, disease, drug, and structure databases from CLI or Python workflows.
skill.summary: Query genomic databases and build reproducible bioinformatics workflows with gget.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical-looking patterns, but review found they are mostly markdown command examples, database URLs, parameter names, and scientific workflow text. No prompt injection attempt, hidden credential exfiltration, or malicious execution path was found. The skill still warrants a medium warning because normal use sends biological data to external services, may use API keys, downloads dependencies, and writes local result files.

medium_findings.title: Expected Network Access to Scientific and AI Services
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally directs users to query public genomic databases and optional AI services. This is core functionality, but user sequences, gene lists, or prompts may leave the local environment when commands are run.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly lists REST and FTP access, and the scripts call gget functions that query external services. This appears intentional rather than covert.

medium_findings.title: API Key Passed in Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The GPT module examples show an API key supplied as a command-line argument and Python parameter. This is not malicious, but command-line secrets can be exposed through shell history, process listings, or shared notebooks.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines directly document an api_key parameter and CLI usage. I did not find evidence that the skill reads environment variables or exfiltrates stored credentials.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Patterns Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The many external command and weak-crypto alerts are false positives from markdown code fences, CLI parameter tables, scientific module names, and database descriptions. They are not hidden execution hooks or obfuscated payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown examples and parameter tables. I found no evidence that these examples execute automatically during skill loading.

low_findings.title: Local File Writes Use User-Controlled Names
low_findings.description: The helper scripts write CSV, FASTA, and alignment files using output prefixes, output directories, and FASTA sequence IDs. This is normal for analysis scripts, but untrusted inputs could create unexpected paths or overwrite local files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly write files from configurable names. The risk is bounded because writes are part of explicit local workflows and there is no evidence of stealthy persistence.

low_findings.title: Optional Setup Downloads Large Dependencies
low_findings.description: The setup section documents dependency downloads for AlphaFold, CELLxGENE, ELM, and GPT integration. Users should review storage, licensing, and network implications before running setup commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The setup commands and dependency list are explicit in the documentation. This is expected functionality, but it changes the local environment when used.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Passed in Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The GPT module examples show an API key supplied as a command-line argument and Python parameter. This is not malicious, but command-line secrets can be exposed through shell history, process listings, or shared notebooks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines directly document an api_key parameter and CLI usage. I did not find evidence that the skill reads environment variables or exfiltrates stored credentials.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Writes Use User-Controlled Names
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts write CSV, FASTA, and alignment files using output prefixes, output directories, and FASTA sequence IDs. This is normal for analysis scripts, but untrusted inputs could create unexpected paths or overwrite local files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly write files from configurable names. The risk is bounded because writes are part of explicit local workflows and there is no evidence of stealthy persistence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional Setup Downloads Large Dependencies
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup section documents dependency downloads for AlphaFold, CELLxGENE, ELM, and GPT integration. Users should review storage, licensing, and network implications before running setup commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The setup commands and dependency list are explicit in the documentation. This is expected functionality, but it changes the local environment when used.

content.user_title: Analyze Genes and Genomic Data with gget
content.value_statement: Bioinformatics work often requires fast queries across many changing public databases. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use gget for gene lookup, sequence analysis, enrichment, and reproducible outputs.
actual_capabilities: Explains gget CLI and Python usage for gene search, metadata lookup, sequence retrieval, and reference genome downloads.
Guides BLAST, BLAT, DIAMOND, MUSCLE, and sequence mutation workflows with documented parameters and output formats.
Supports enrichment, expression, OpenTargets, cBioPortal, COSMIC, AlphaFold, and CELLxGENE workflow planning.
Includes scripts for single-gene analysis, enrichment pipelines, and batch sequence analysis using the gget package.
Documents database sources, update patterns, setup requirements, output formats, and reproducibility practices.
limitations: It depends on external databases whose schemas, availability, and content can change over time.
Some modules require internet access, accounts, API keys, large downloads, or extra local dependencies.
The included scripts are examples and may need validation before use with regulated or sensitive datasets.
It does not replace specialist review for biological interpretation, clinical claims, or publication-quality analysis.

use_cases.title: Rapid Gene Lookup
use_cases.description: Find gene identifiers, annotations, sequences, protein data, and disease associations from public databases.

use_cases.title: Sequence Analysis Workflow
use_cases.description: Plan BLAST searches, align sequences, retrieve FASTA outputs, and organize local results for follow-up analysis.

use_cases.title: Enrichment and Target Discovery
use_cases.description: Run enrichment, expression, pathway, disease, and drug association workflows for candidate gene lists.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Gene
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the gget skill to find the best gget commands for looking up TP53 in human and saving the main identifiers.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve Sequences
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use gget to retrieve nucleotide and protein sequences for an Ensembl gene ID and save reproducible FASTA outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Enrichment Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a gget enrichment workflow for a human gene list, including database choices, output files, and checks for failed queries.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Multi-Database Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reproducible gget workflow that combines gene metadata, ARCHS4 expression, OpenTargets disease data, and sequence analysis for candidate prioritization.

output_examples.input: Look up TP53 and explain what gget commands I should run.
output_examples.output: A short workflow with search, info, sequence retrieval, and output-saving commands, plus notes about species names and database updates.

output_examples.input: I have a list of genes from RNA-seq. Help me run enrichment analysis.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step enrichment plan covering gene list format, species selection, database shortcuts, saved tables, plots, and interpretation cautions.

output_examples.input: Compare options for sequence similarity analysis in gget.
output_examples.output: A practical comparison of BLAST, BLAT, DIAMOND, and MUSCLE use cases with expected inputs and outputs.

best_practices: Run gget in a clean virtual environment and record package and database versions for reproducibility.
Save raw outputs with clear prefixes, then review results before using them in reports or downstream pipelines.
Use rate limiting and caching when querying many genes or large public databases.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys into shared chat transcripts, shell history, notebooks, or version-controlled files.
Do not run large downloads or AlphaFold setup without checking storage, runtime, and dependency requirements.
Do not treat database scores or enrichment terms as clinical evidence without independent expert validation.

faq.question: What is gget used for?
faq.answer: gget provides CLI and Python access to genomic, protein, expression, enrichment, disease, and structure databases.

faq.question: Does this skill run analysis by itself?
faq.answer: It provides instructions, examples, and helper scripts. Users still choose inputs, run commands, and review outputs.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: Most gget modules query public web services. Some setup modules also download dependencies or databases.

faq.question: Can it handle API keys?
faq.answer: The documentation covers modules that accept API keys. Users should store secrets securely and avoid command history exposure.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it suitable for clinical decisions?
faq.answer: No. It can support research workflows, but clinical interpretation requires validated methods and qualified review.
